THE SUDDEN FALL OF OPENAI'S MOST HYPED PRODUCT SINCE CHATGPT
articles.summary: Sora was hyped as AI's next consumer-friendly frontier, and even Disney signed onto the vision. OpenAI then suddenly killed the project, with many Disney executives learning about the decision less than an hour before it was announced. OpenAI needed to free up more computing resources, and Sora was using far too much. The product wasn't profitable, and every user drew down a finite resource. CEO Sam Altman says the move was a difficult but necessary sacrifice toward the company's larger goals.

articles.title: APPLE PIVOTS ITS AI STRATEGY TO APP STORE, SEARCH-LIKE PLATFORM APPROACH
articles.summary: Apple is recommitting to its core business model of selling hardware and services. It knows its homegrown AI technology lags behind competitors, so it is focusing on its core strengths. Historically, Apple's software has been about driving product sales rather than generating revenue in their own right. It plans to embed just enough AI in its OSes to keep users from defecting, while opening Siri and Apple Intelligence to third-party services. This approach leverages its hardware, makes its products more customizable, and keeps the company in control of its ecosystem.

articles.title: INSIDE THE STEALTHY STARTUP THAT PITCHED BRAINLESS HUMAN CLONES
articles.summary: A Californian startup called R3 has raised funds to create non-sentient monkey 'organ sacks' as an alternative to animal testing. The startup's founder reportedly secretly pitched a vision where the startup creates 'brainless clones' to serve as backups for human bodies. This could be in the form of a baby version of a person with enough brain structure to be alive in case they ever need a new kidney or liver, or it may even be possible to one day transplant a brain into a younger clone to gain a second lifespan. The company has denied that it ever made the pitch, saying that any allegations of intent or conspiracy to create human clones or humans with brain damage are categorically false.

articles.title: VULNERABILITY RESEARCH IS COOKED
articles.summary: Coding agents will soon drastically alter both the practice and the economics of exploit development. A substantial amount of the high-impact vulnerability research will just be pointing an agent at a source three and telling it to find zero days. This will profoundly alter information security and the Internet itself.

CODEX PLUGIN FOR CLAUDE CODE
articles.summary: This plugin enables Claude Code users to start using Codex from the workflow they already have. Users can use Codex from inside Claude Code for code reviews or delegate tasks to Codex. The plugin uses the local Codex CLI, so existing sign-in methods and configs still apply. It also uses the same repository checkout and machine-local environment.

articles.title: ENGINEERING FOR AGENTS THAT NEVER SLEEP
articles.summary: Most of the signals that trigger engineering work are already machine-readable. The current bottleneck is where a human reads that signal, interprets it, and then types a prompt telling the agent what the signal already said. Give agents comprehensive unit tests so they can verify their own output, good documentation to discover context, reproducible dev environments to run code, and rich system accounting. This scaffolding will enable agents that can close incidents around the clock.

articles.title: TO LURE TOP AI TALENT, STARTUPS ARE TURNING TO COLD HARD CASH
articles.summary: High-growth AI startups are flush with venture capital. Combined with a wildly competitive talent market, this has caused increasingly creative incentive structures and more cash-heavy offers. Startups are focusing on hiring the best as they need to stay lean and small. This is creating a split economy in the tech industry where the top 5% to 10% of candidates are getting all of the offers and the rest are struggling.

articles.title: SEEING LIKE A SPREADSHEET
articles.summary: AI will see much further than the spreadsheet ever could. It will allow businesses to accomplish incredible things, and make possible coordinations of labor and capital more ambitious than anything we can imagine today. However, as corporations become dominated by AI systems, the most human elements of organizations may be devalued or discarded entirely. Great organizations are great because of something irreducible about the collection and organization of particular people toward particular ends.

articles.title: COST TO IMPLEMENT VS COST TO VERIFY
articles.summary: The cost to implement is the time and expertise needed to produce code, and the cost to verify is the time and expertise needed to confirm the code is correct.
